title: Position Sizing and Risk of Ruin
html:
The first job is survival

Most beginners obsess over entries — the perfect setup, the magic indicator. Professionals obsess over size. You can be right about direction and still go broke if you bet too much on any single trade. Position sizing is the discipline that keeps a string of losses — which is statistically guaranteed to happen — from ending your career. The math is simple, and ignoring it is the single most common reason traders blow up.

The fixed-fractional rule.
Risk a small fixed fraction of your account equity on each trade — commonly 0.5% to 2%. Shares = (Account × Risk%) ÷ (Entry − Stop). The dollars you risk stay proportional to your account, so losses shrink as you draw down and gains compound as you grow.

The core worked example

You have a $50,000 account and risk 1% per trade. That is $500 of risk — not $500 of position, but the maximum you intend to lose if the stop is hit. You buy ACME at $40 with a stop at $38, so your per-share risk is $2.

1% sizing on a $2 stop
Account equity $50,000 Risk per trade (1%) $50,000 × 0.01 = $500 Entry − Stop $40 − $38 = $2 per share Shares $500 ÷ $2 = 250 shares Position value 250 × $40 = $10,000 (20% of account) If stopped out 250 × $2 = −$500 (−1%)

Notice the position is $10,000 — a fifth of the account — yet you only risk $500. The stop distance, not the position size, controls your risk. A tight $1 stop would let you buy 500 shares; a wide $5 stop only 100 shares. Same $500 at risk every time. This is the key insight: let the stop set the size, never the other way around.

Above, the entry is near $40 with a structural stop at $38. If price breaks down to the stop, the loss is contained to a known, pre-budgeted $500. You decided the size before you clicked buy, when you were calm.

Why 2% bets are more than twice as dangerous

Risk of ruin is the probability that a losing streak draws your account down to a level you cannot recover from. It rises non-linearly with bet size because drawdowns compound. Lose 1% ten times in a row and you are down ~9.6%. Lose 2% ten times and you are down ~18.3% — nearly double, and the deeper hole demands a bigger percentage gain to climb out.

The drawdown asymmetry
Lose 10% → need to recover +11.1% Lose 25% → need to recover +33.3% Lose 50% → need to recover +100% Lose 75% → need to recover +300%

A 50% loss requires a 100% gain just to break even. This is why big bets are poison: a single bad streak forces a recovery so large that most accounts never make it back. Cutting size from 2% to 1% does not merely halve the pain — it dramatically collapses the tail probability of an unrecoverable drawdown.

The ruin trap.
With a 50% win rate, an 8-loss streak happens roughly once every 256 sequences — common over a trading career. At 2% per loss that is a ~15% drawdown; pile on a few more and you are in the danger zone. The trader who risks 5% per trade and hits that same streak is down ~34% and emotionally wrecked.

Practical guardrails

  • Beginners: risk 0.25%–0.5% while you are still learning whether your edge is real. You are paying tuition; pay it in small bills.
  • Established edge: 1% is the durable workhorse. It survives long losing streaks and still compounds.
  • Never average: adding to a loser to lower your cost basis silently doubles your risk past the budget. The stop is the stop.
▸Try It

Build your own sizing table for a $25,000 account at 1% risk.

  1. Compute your dollar risk per trade (account × 0.01).
  2. For stop distances of $0.50, $1, $2, and $4, calculate shares = $250 ÷ distance.
  3. Note the position value in each case and confirm the dollar risk stays constant at $250.
  4. Now redo it at 2% and compare how much faster a 10-loss streak would draw you down.

title: Risk/Reward and Expectancy
html:
Win rate is a vanity metric

New traders chase high win rates and feel proud of being right 70% of the time — then lose money. The reason is that how much you make when right and how much you lose when wrong matters more than how often. The two numbers that actually decide your fate are risk/reward per trade and expectancy across many trades. Master these and you can be wrong most of the time and still get rich.

Risk/reward (R:R)

R:R compares your potential reward to your risk on a single trade. Define one unit of risk as R = the distance from entry to stop. Reward is the distance from entry to target. A trade that risks $2 to make $4 is a 2:1 trade, or a 2R target.

Computing R:R on a long
Entry $50.00 Stop $48.00 → risk (1R) = $2.00 Target $54.00 → reward = $4.00 Risk/reward $4 ÷ $2 = 2.0 (a 2R trade)

The chart shows the geometry: a small fixed downside to the stop and double the room to the target. Demanding a minimum R:R — say, no trade below 2:1 — is a filter that quietly does most of the work. It forces you to skip marginal setups and only deploy capital where the payoff justifies the risk.

Expectancy: the only number that compounds

Expectancy is the average profit (in R) you expect per trade, blending win rate and R:R. It tells you whether your system has a real mathematical edge.

Expectancy formula.
Expectancy (in R) = (Win% × AvgWinR) − (Loss% × AvgLossR). A positive number means each trade, on average, adds value. Multiply by your dollar risk to get expected dollars per trade.
50% win rate at 2:1 reward
Win probability 0.50, average win = +2R Loss probability 0.50, average loss = −1R Expectancy (0.50 × 2) − (0.50 × 1) = +0.50R At $500 risk/trade +0.50R × $500 = +$250 per trade (avg)

Half the time you lose; you still make $250 per trade on average. Over 200 trades that is roughly +$50,000 of expected edge before compounding. This is the engine of professional trading: a modest positive expectancy applied with discipline over a large sample.

Why high win rates can lose money

Consider a trader who wins 70% of the time but only takes 0.5:1 trades — risking $2 to make $1 — because cutting winners early feels good.

70% win rate at 0.5:1 — a losing system
Wins 0.70 × (+0.5R) = +0.35R Losses 0.30 × (−1R) = −0.30R Expectancy +0.35 − 0.30 = +0.05R (barely positive) After ~$0.05R in costs/slippage ≈ 0R or negative

Being right 70% of the time produces almost no edge here, and trading costs push it underwater. Meanwhile the 50%-win, 2:1 trader prints +0.50R. It is the size of the wins relative to the losses that builds wealth, not the frequency of being right.

Insight.
Expectancy × number of trades × dollar risk = your expected profit. Improve any of the three: raise expectancy (better setups/exits), trade more (within your edge), or — carefully — size up once the edge is proven. The first is by far the safest lever.
Sample-size warning.
A handful of trades tells you nothing. Expectancy is only meaningful over 50–100+ trades. A short hot streak can mask a negative-expectancy system, and a short cold streak can hide a great one. Judge the process, not the last five outcomes.
▸Try It

Compute expectancy for three hypothetical systems.

  1. System A: 40% win rate, 3:1 reward. Calculate (0.40×3) − (0.60×1).
  2. System B: 60% win rate, 1:1 reward.
  3. System C: 35% win rate, 2:1 reward.
  4. Rank them by expectancy in R, then by expected dollars if you risk $400 per trade. Which surprised you?

title: Where to Place Stops and How to Exit
html:
The exit is the trade

Your entry gets all the attention, but your exits determine your actual P&L. A stop that is too tight gets shaken out by noise; one that is too loose bleeds capital. And on the winning side, the difference between a mediocre and an exceptional trader is almost entirely how they handle the exit — whether they bank a small gain in fear or let a winner run into a multi-R home run.

Place stops at structure, not at round numbers

A stop should sit at a price where your trade thesis is proven wrong, not at an arbitrary dollar amount. For a long, that is typically just below a swing low, a support shelf, or a moving average the trend has been respecting. The logic: if price violates that structure, the reason you entered no longer holds.

Structural stop.
Long: place the stop a small buffer below the most recent higher-low / support. Short: place it above the most recent lower-high / resistance. Add an ATR-based buffer so normal volatility does not clip you — e.g. stop = swing low − (0.5 × ATR).

In the chart the entry is on a pullback within an uptrend. The swing low is the line in the sand — below it the higher-high/higher-low structure breaks. Placing the stop just beneath it, plus a fraction of ATR, keeps you in the trade through ordinary wiggles while still defining a clean invalidation point. Remember: you set size from this stop distance (Lesson 13.1), so a structurally-correct stop and correct sizing are the same decision.

Avoid the obvious stop.
Do not cluster your stop at the exact round number or the exact prior low where everyone else parks theirs — those pools of stops are precisely where price gets hunted. A small buffer below the herd often saves the trade.

Scaling out: partials and runners

You rarely have to be all-in or all-out. A robust exit plan banks some profit early to reduce risk, then lets a remainder ride for outsized gains. A common structure: exit half at 2R, move the stop to breakeven, and trail the rest.

Partial at 2R + trailing runner
Entry $50, stop $48 (1R = $2), size 250 sh risk = $500 Price hits 2R = $54 sell 125 sh → +$500 banked Move stop on remaining 125 to $50 trade now risk-free Runner trails up, exits at $60 (5R) 125 × $10 = +$1,250 Total on the trade +$1,750 = +3.5R

After the partial at $54 you have already covered your initial risk; the stop to breakeven means the worst case is now a scratch. The runner then captures a large move you would have missed by exiting everything at 2R. This structure converts the occasional trend into the outsized winners that carry an entire month — without forcing you to predict the top.

Trailing methods

  • Swing-low trail: ratchet the stop up under each new higher-low. Simple and respects market structure.
  • ATR / Chandelier trail: stop = highest high since entry − (3 × ATR). Adapts to volatility, gives trends room to breathe.
  • Moving-average trail: exit on a close below the 20-EMA. Good for smooth trends, but lags at sharp tops.

Here the Parabolic SAR dots flip below price during the trend, giving a clear trailing-stop trigger, while the 21-EMA provides a smoother alternative. Both let profits run while defining the point where you step aside. The right choice depends on temperament: tighter trails bank more often but cut winners short; looser trails give back more but capture the big moves.

Insight.
Decide all three exits — stop, first target, and trail rule — before you enter, and write them down. In the heat of a moving market your judgment is compromised; pre-committed exits let your calm self trade for your panicked self.
▸Try It

Design a full exit plan for one paper trade.

  1. Pick a chart and mark the swing low; set your stop a half-ATR below it.
  2. Define a 2R target and decide what fraction you will sell there.
  3. Choose a trailing rule (swing-low, ATR, or MA) for the runner and write the exact trigger.
  4. Track how the trade would have played out and compare total R against simply exiting all at 2R.

title: Portfolio Risk, Correlation and Diversification
html:
Your real risk is the sum, not the parts

You can size every individual trade perfectly at 1% and still be carrying catastrophic risk. Why? Because positions do not move independently. If five of your 1% trades all rise and fall together, you do not have five small bets — you have one 5% bet wearing five tickers. Portfolio-level risk management is about controlling the aggregate exposure that correlation creates.

Correlation: the hidden multiplier

Correlation (ρ) measures how two assets move together, ranging from +1 (lockstep) to −1 (perfect opposites), with 0 meaning unrelated. When you hold several highly-correlated positions, their risks add up rather than cancel out.

Five 'separate' tech longs
5 positions, each risking 1% feels like 'diversified' All mega-cap tech, ρ ≈ 0.9 they move as one Sector sells off → all 5 hit stops −1% × 5 = −5% in a day Effective single bet ~5% account risk, not 1%

This is the trap. The trader believed they spread risk across five names, but a single sector headline — a rate surprise, a regulatory shock — knocks all five down together. The portfolio behaves like one oversized position. Genuine diversification requires positions whose drivers are different.

Correlation regime shift.
In a crisis, correlations spike toward +1. Assets that looked unrelated in calm markets all crash together when liquidity vanishes. Size for the correlated case, not the calm case — your diversification disappears exactly when you need it most.

How diversification actually reduces risk

The benefit comes from imperfect correlation. Two positions with ρ below 1 partly offset each other's swings, so portfolio volatility is less than the simple sum of the parts.

Two-asset portfolio risk.
σp = √(w12σ12 + w22σ22 + 2w1w2ρσ1σ2). When ρ = 1 the risks add fully; as ρ falls, the cross term shrinks and total risk drops below the sum.
Two equal 10%-vol positions
ρ = +1.0 (identical bet) σp = 10.0% — no benefit ρ = +0.5 σp ≈ 8.7% ρ = 0 (unrelated) σp ≈ 7.1% ρ = −0.5 (offsetting) σp ≈ 5.0%

At ρ = 0 the two positions together carry only ~71% of the volatility of putting it all in one — diversification gave you a free reduction in risk for the same expected return. At ρ = −0.5 it drops to half. This is the closest thing to a free lunch in markets, but it only exists when your positions are genuinely driven by different forces.

Practical portfolio rules

  • Cap total open risk. Sum the risk across all open trades and keep it under a ceiling — e.g. 5%–6% of equity at any time. If you are already at the cap, a new idea must replace an old one, not stack on top.
  • Group by correlation, not by ticker. Treat all your tech longs, or all your USD-sensitive trades, as one bucket and limit risk per bucket (e.g. 2% per theme).
  • Watch the net directional tilt. Ten longs and zero shorts in a bull market is a leveraged bet on the index, however clever each name looks.
  • Stress test. Ask: if my main theme drops 10% overnight, what is my total loss? If the answer scares you, you are too concentrated.
Insight.
Diversification is not owning many things — it is owning many uncorrelated things. Twenty crypto tokens is one bet. A stock, a bond, a commodity, and a currency pair driven by different macro forces is real diversification.
▸Try It

Audit a hypothetical 6-position portfolio for hidden concentration.

  1. List six positions you might realistically hold and their individual 1% risks.
  2. Group them into correlation buckets (sector, factor, currency, direction).
  3. Estimate your true exposure per bucket and your worst-case loss if the largest bucket drops together.
  4. Rebalance on paper so no single theme exceeds 2% of account risk.

title: The Psychology: Fear, Greed, Tilt and Biases
html:
The market is a mirror

You can know the math of sizing and expectancy cold and still lose, because in the moment of decision your emotions override your rules. Fear makes you cut winners early and freeze on entries; greed makes you oversize and chase. The losing trader is rarely defeated by the market — they are defeated by their own nervous system. Learning to recognize these states is the difference between a plan on paper and a plan that survives contact with real money.

The anatomy of a tilt spiral

The single most destructive sequence in trading starts with loss aversion — the well-documented finding that losses hurt about twice as much as equivalent gains feel good. That asymmetry drives a predictable, account-ending chain.

The tilt cascade
1. Price hits the stop 'It will come back — I'll give it room' 2. Stop cancelled, loss grows −1R becomes −3R 3. Position finally closed in pain realized 3× the planned loss 4. Anger → revenge trade oversized, no setup, 'get it back' 5. Revenge trade also loses −6R day from one −1R mistake

Every step is an emotional decision dressed as a rational one. Holding past the stop is loss aversion refusing to accept a sure small loss in exchange for a chance to avoid it. The revenge trade is tilt — the poker term for letting a bad outcome corrupt your judgment. A disciplined −1R becomes a −6R catastrophe, not because the analysis was wrong, but because the trader could not feel a small loss without trying to erase it.

Tilt is physical.
After a painful loss your body floods with cortisol and adrenaline; your prefrontal cortex — the part that does math and follows rules — is literally less active. You are not the same trader you were an hour ago. The only reliable defense is a pre-committed rule that removes the decision: walk away.

The cognitive biases that wreck accounts

Beyond raw emotion, a set of systematic thinking errors quietly degrades decisions. Naming them is the first step to catching them in real time.

  • Loss aversion: holding losers too long, cutting winners too soon — the exact opposite of positive expectancy.
  • Confirmation bias: seeking only news and chart reads that support your open position while ignoring the warning signs.
  • Recency bias: over-weighting the last few trades — going timid after losses or reckless after wins, when each trade is statistically independent.
  • Sunk-cost fallacy: 'I've already lost so much here, I can't sell now' — past losses are irrelevant to the forward decision.
  • Anchoring: fixating on your entry price or a past high as 'fair value,' refusing to act until price returns there.
  • Overconfidence / house money: after a winning streak, sizing up and skipping checks because you feel invincible. Streaks end; oversized positions during them do real damage.
  • FOMO: chasing a move already extended, buying the top because watching others profit is unbearable.
Insight.
You cannot eliminate these biases — they are hardwired. You can only build systems that act before the bias does: hard stops entered at trade time, position sizes calculated in advance, and a checklist that must be satisfied before any click. The goal is to make the disciplined action the default, requiring effort to override rather than effort to follow.

Greed: the bull-market killer

Fear gets the headlines, but greed empties more accounts in good times. After a few wins, the temptation to risk 3% instead of 1%, to add a sixth correlated position, or to hold past the target 'because it keeps going' feels like confidence. It is actually the setup for giving back weeks of gains in a single overexposed day. The most dangerous moment in trading is right after a winning streak, when your guard is down and your size is up.

Definition — tilt.
A state of emotional, reactive trading triggered by a loss, a streak, or frustration, in which decisions are driven by the urge to recover or to feel right rather than by the trading plan. Detectable signs: oversizing, trading setups you would normally skip, abandoning stops, and a racing pulse.
▸Try It

Build your personal tilt early-warning system.

  1. Recall your last three painful trading decisions and label the bias behind each (loss aversion, FOMO, revenge, etc.).
  2. Write down your personal physical tilt signals (clenched jaw, refreshing the chart constantly, anger).
  3. Define a single hard rule: 'When I notice X, I close the platform for the rest of the day.'
  4. Post it where you trade and commit to it for one week.

title: Building Rules and Discipline
html:
Discipline is a system, not a virtue

Telling yourself to 'be more disciplined' fails for the same reason resolutions fail: it relies on willpower, which collapses under stress — exactly when you need it. The professional solution is to pre-commit. You make your important decisions in advance, while calm, and encode them as concrete rules and routines. Then in the heat of the moment you are not deciding; you are merely executing. Discipline becomes an environment you build, not a muscle you strain.

The pre-trade checklist

A checklist forces every trade through the same gate, stripping out impulse and FOMO. If a trade cannot pass every line, you do not take it — no exceptions, no 'just this once.' Surgeons and pilots use checklists for the same reason: under pressure, smart people skip obvious steps.

Pre-trade checklist (must be ALL yes)
✓ Setup matches a defined strategy in my plan ✓ Higher-timeframe trend agrees with the trade ✓ R:R is at least 2:1 to a logical target ✓ Stop is at structure; size calculated for 1% risk ✓ Total open portfolio risk stays under 5% ✓ I am calm, not chasing or revenge-trading ✗ Any 'no' → no trade. Walk away.

The power of the checklist is that it converts vague judgment into a binary gate. The marginal trades — the ones taken out of boredom or fear of missing out — are precisely the ones that fail the list, and they are precisely the ones that drain accounts. Filtering them out raises your average trade quality without you having to be more talented, only more consistent.

The hard daily-loss limit

The most important rule a trader can have is a circuit breaker: a fixed maximum loss per day after which you stop, no matter what. This single rule prevents the tilt cascade from Lesson 13.5 — it caps a bad day before it becomes a career-ending day.

The −3R circuit breaker
Daily loss limit −3R (three full stop-outs) Trade 1 −1R Trade 2 −1R (running −2R) Trade 3 −1R → hit −3R limit Action platform closed, done for the day Worst case protected −3R, never −10R revenge spiral

Without the limit, that third loss is the trigger for tilt — the revenge trade, the oversize, the cancelled stop. With it, −3R is simply the worst your day can be. You lived to trade tomorrow with a clear head and an intact account. Over a career, surviving the bad days matters more than maximizing the good ones, because one uncapped day can erase a year.

Pre-commitment.
The practice of locking in a decision in advance so your future, emotionally-compromised self cannot override it. Examples: bracket orders that auto-place the stop, a daily-loss limit enforced by your broker's lockout, and a written plan reviewed before the session.

Routines that build the habit

  • Pre-market routine: review the plan, mark levels, check the economic calendar, set your max-loss limit for the day before the open.
  • In-session discipline: place the stop the instant you enter — use a bracket/OCO order so the exit exists before emotion can interfere.
  • Post-session journal: log every trade with the setup, the R outcome, and — crucially — whether you followed your rules, separate from whether you made money. A rule-following loss is a good trade; a rule-breaking win is a bad trade you got lucky on.
  • Weekly review: tally expectancy, count rule violations, and identify your single most expensive mistake to target next week.
Insight.
Grade yourself on process, not P&L. Outcomes are noisy over any short window; rule-adherence is fully in your control. A trader who follows their rules 95% of the time, even through a losing week, is winning. The money follows the process, not the other way around.
The 'just this once' trap.
Every blown account has a moment where the trader broke one rule 'just this once' and it worked — teaching the brain that breaking rules pays. That lucky win is more dangerous than a loss, because it trains the exact behavior that eventually ruins you. Defend your rules hardest when breaking them would have worked.
▸Try It

Draft your personal rulebook.

  1. Write a 5-line pre-trade checklist tailored to your strategy; every line must be answerable yes/no.
  2. Set a hard daily-loss limit in R and decide the exact action when you hit it.
  3. Create a one-line journal template that records setup, R result, and rule-adherence (yes/no).
  4. Trade ten paper trades using all three and review how often you would have followed them.

title: Writing a Concrete Trading Plan
html:
If it is not written, it does not exist

Everything in this part — sizing, expectancy, stops, portfolio limits, psychology, rules — only becomes operational when it is assembled into a single written trading plan. A plan in your head is not a plan; it is a mood that changes with the market. A written plan is a contract with yourself that you can be held to and, just as importantly, that you can review and improve. The best traders treat their plan as a living document, refined over hundreds of trades.

What a complete plan specifies

A good plan answers, in advance, every decision you would otherwise make under pressure. It does not need to be long — one page is ideal — but it must be concrete. 'Buy strong stocks' is not a plan; 'buy the first pullback to the 20-EMA in an uptrend, entering on the reclaim of the prior day's high' is.

One-page trading plan template
MARKET Liquid US large-cap stocks, daily timeframe EDGE Trend-pullback continuation SETUP Uptrend (price > rising 50-SMA), pullback to 20-EMA TRIGGER Entry on reclaim of prior-day high RISK 1% of equity per trade; max 5% open STOP 0.5 ATR below the pullback swing low TARGETS Half at 2R, trail remainder under swing lows LIMITS Stop trading at −3R on the day REVIEW Journal daily, tally expectancy weekly

Notice how each line maps to a lesson in this part: RISK to position sizing (13.1), the 2R target to risk/reward (13.2), the structural stop and trailing exit to 13.3, the 5% open cap to portfolio risk (13.4), the daily limit to discipline (13.5–13.6). The plan is the integration point where all the theory becomes a single repeatable procedure.

The chart shows the plan executing exactly as written. Price holds above the rising 50-SMA (trend filter), pulls back to the 21-EMA (setup), then reclaims the prior-day high (trigger). The stop sits below the swing low, defining the 1R that sets the share count. Because every step was specified in advance, the actual trade requires zero improvisation — you are simply matching reality to your checklist and acting.

A worked plan-to-trade walkthrough

Plan applied: APEX trade on a $40,000 account
Risk budget (1%) $40,000 × 0.01 = $400 Trigger fills at $100.00 Stop (0.5 ATR below swing low) $96.00 → 1R = $4 Size $400 ÷ $4 = 100 shares Target 1 (2R = $108) sell 50 sh → +$400, stop to breakeven Runner trails, exits $116 (4R) 50 × $16 = +$800 Trade result +$1,200 = +3R, fully rule-based

Every number flows mechanically from the plan. There was no moment of 'how much should I buy?' or 'should I take profits?' — those answers existed before the trade. That is the entire point: the plan does your thinking when thinking is hardest.

Definition — trading plan.
A written document specifying the markets you trade, your edge, exact entry/exit/sizing rules, risk limits, and review cadence — designed so that any given trade can be executed without discretionary judgment in the moment.
Insight.
Your first plan will be imperfect, and that is fine. The value is not in writing the perfect plan but in having a plan you can test against reality, measure with your journal, and refine. A mediocre plan followed consistently beats a brilliant plan abandoned under stress every single time.
Don't over-edit mid-stream.
Change your plan during scheduled reviews with a sample of trades behind you — never mid-trade or right after a loss. Tweaking rules in reaction to the last outcome is just tilt with extra steps; it destroys the consistency that makes the plan measurable.
▸Try It

Write your own one-page trading plan.

  1. Fill in every line of the template: market, edge, setup, trigger, risk%, stop logic, targets, daily limit, review cadence.
  2. Make each line concrete enough that a stranger could execute it without asking you a question.
  3. Run one real chart through it end-to-end, computing size and the full exit sequence as in the worked example.
  4. Commit to following it unchanged for 20 trades, then schedule a review to refine it.
